EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Fire suppression activities continued on the Big Hollow wildfire as cooler and wetter weather continued.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Hollow fire was declared contained on November 13, 2020 at 0900PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Fire suppression activities continued on the Big Hollow wildfire as cooler and wetter weather continued.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Hollow fire was declared contained on November 13, 2020 at 0900PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of strong Pacific fronts moved across the region bringing high winds to the coast with heavy rain across much of the area. The heavy rains resulted in flooding of some coastal rivers as well as small stream flooding and a debris flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Megler Bridge sensor (ODT76) measured wind speed of 55 mph gusting to 68 mph.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF PIERCE AND LEWIS COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant weather system impacted western Washington over the course of several days, bringing breezy winds, landslides, and river flooding to the lowlands, and heavy snow to parts of the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant snowfall began to fall across the Southern Cascades around noon on the 4th. Between 20-23 inches were then recorded near Mount Rainier National Park 24 hours later on the 5th. More snow continued thereafter before eventually tapering. Total new snowfall from this event near Paradise was between 40-45 inches.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF PIERCE AND LEWIS COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall affected the Southern Cascades for several days in Mid-February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began falling across the Southern Cascades on the 13th, with the most significant snowfall recorded on the 15th. Snow then leveled off through the night into the 16th.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF WHATCOM AND SKAGIT COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An additional round of impactful snowfall was recorded at the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Another quick shot of heavy snowfall commenced across the Northern Cascades during the evening hours of the 28th. By daybreak the next morning, 12 new inches of snow were reported at Mount Baker Ski Area. Snow then quickly tapered through the morning hours of the 29th.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF SNOHOMISH AND KING COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant weather system impacted western Washington over the course of several days, bringing breezy winds, landslides, and river flooding to the lowlands, and heavy snow to parts of the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began to fall across the Central Cascades during the evening hours of the 4th, with decent snowfall accumulation recorded at Stevens Pass by daybreak on the 5th. The most significant snowfall from this storm fell on the 6th, with 16 inches of additional snowfall reported in a 12 hour period. A snow slide occurred at Stevens Pass on the 6th as heavy snow fell, causing the Pass to be closed in both directions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A landspout tornado touched down in Richland, WA on the afternoon of March 31st in an open area. The tornado was captured on video and appeared to be quite weak. No damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple public videos of a weak landspout tornado on the north side of Richland, WA in an open area. No damage reported.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF WHATCOM AND SKAGIT COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several periods of heavy snow fell across the Northern Cascades towards the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began falling across the Northern Cascades during the early morning hours of the 22nd, with only several inches of snow accumulation affecting Mount Baker Ski Area into the morning hours of the 23rd. Thereafter, significant snow began to fall, with 22 inches of new snow recorded by the morning of the 24th. Snow then began to taper into the 25th with little additional snow accumulation.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE WASHINGTON CASCADES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy Snow impacted the Washington Cascades and Blue Mountains with 1-2 feet of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twenty-four inches of snow in Roslyn, 20 inches  in Easton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy Snow impacted the Washington Cascades and Blue Mountains with 1-2 feet of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twelve inches of new snow at Ski Bluewood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beginning during the evening of January 15th and continuing through the 16th a warm front pushed north through eastern Washington. The East Slopes of the Cascades and the adjacent Columbia Basin zones were under the deepest moisture feed and received heavy snow accumulations. The remainder of the Columbia Basin received generally 1 to 3 inches of snow accumulation. The warm front stalled near the Canadian border which promoted a longer period of snow over the northern mountains of eastern Washington where another region of heavy accumulations resulted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer in East Wenatchee recorded 5.5 inches of new snow accumulation from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front briefly dropped down into eastern Washington and north Idaho on January 13th and 14th. Light snow accompanied this frontal passage with generally 1 to 3 inches of snow accumulation around the region. However the front stalled along the Washington/Oregon border and promoted an extended period of light snow which left a swath of heavier accumulations From the Blue Mountains through the Lewiston, ID area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer located 14 miles south of Asotin recorded 13.0 inches of snow accumulation form this storm.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF PIERCE AND LEWIS COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a period of light snow at the very beginning of the month, a more significant winter storm brought heavier snowfall accumulations to the mountains beginning January 4th and continuing through January 8th, prompting the issuance of a Winter Storm Warning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began to fall late in the evening hours on the 4th, with 13 inches of fresh snow at Paradise by the afternoon of the 4th. Thereafter, periods of heavy snow persisted across the southern Cascades into the early afternoon hours on the 8th, bringing the storm total snowfall to around 65 inches for Paradise and nearby locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a period of light snow at the very beginning of the month, a more significant winter storm brought heavier snowfall accumulations to the mountains beginning January 4th and continuing through January 8th, prompting the issuance of a Winter Storm Warning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began to fall around midnight on the 4th and continued through the morning hours of the 7th, with total snowfall near Hurricane Ridge around 22 inches.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF SNOHOMISH AND KING COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a period of light snow at the very beginning of the month, a more significant winter storm brought heavier snowfall accumulations to the mountains beginning January 4th and continuing through January 8th, prompting the issuance of a Winter Storm Warning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow started in the central Cascades during the morning hours of the 4th and began to accumulate quickly. Snowfall affected both Stevens Pass and Snoqualmie Pass, with snowfall accumulation at Stevens Pass significantly higher than at Snoqualmie Pass. New snowfall at Stevens Pass of around 49 inches by January 8th and around 22 inches at Snoqualmie Pass. Stevens Pass was closed on January 6th.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF PIERCE AND LEWIS COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following closely on the heels of the previous winter storm was another winter storm across the mountainous terrain of western Washington. Periods of heavy snow continued off and on without much in the way of noticeable breaks over the course of a week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began during the early morning hours of the 10th and continued fairly consistently through the 18th. Measurements around Paradise indicated a storm total snowfall of around 39 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following closely on the heels of the previous winter storm was another winter storm across the mountainous terrain of western Washington. Periods of heavy snow continued off and on without much in the way of noticeable breaks over the course of a week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulation began during the late morning hours of the 10th and continued through the 18th off and on, with the highest accumulation between the 15 and the 18th. The equipment near Hurricane Ridge became dysfunctional through the latter half of the event, with estimated storm total snowfall near 40 inches.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF WHATCOM AND SKAGIT COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another weather system brought several days of impactful snow accumulations to the mountains late in the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began during the early afternoon hours on January 21st, with new snow accumulation of around 25 inches by the morning hours of the 22nd. Snow continued over the next several days, eventually tapering on the 24th. Storm total snowfall accumulation around Mount Baker of 38 inches.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF PIERCE AND LEWIS COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another weather system brought another round of heavy mountain snow to Washington at the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began during the very late evening hours on the 27th, with snow continuing through the 30th. New snow accumulation of around 30 inches near Paradise.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front moving in from Canada collided with moisture from a trough off the Pacific Coast from the afternoon of March 13th into the morning of March 14th. Areas of heavy snow fell in the Okanogan Highlands and northern mountains of Washington along the frontal zone. The surrounding zones in the northern Columbia Basin and Okanogan Valley generally received 2 to 4 inches snow accumulation from this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather spotter 6 miles northwest of Diamond Lake reported 17.0 inches of new snow accumulation from this storm.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF WHATCOM AND SKAGIT COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weather system brought snowfall to the Cascades Mountains at the beginning of the month, with the heaviest snowfall impacting the Northern Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began falling during the afternoon hours of the 5th and by the early overnight hours, around 14 inches of new snow accumulated at Mount Baker Ski Area. Snow then began to taper off through the afternoon hours of the 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a cold front moving through eastern Washington during the morning of March 30th. Down burst winds from a storm along this front downed a few trees in the Spokane and Diamond Lake of Northeast Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported a tree downed on a house on Indian Trail Road in Northwest Spokane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a cold front moving through eastern Washington during the morning of March 30th. Down burst winds from a storm along this front downed a few trees in the Spokane and Diamond Lake of Northeast Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported downed trees and power lines on Green Road in the Diamond Lake area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the day of February 4th light snow developed along a stationary front laying west to east across central Washington and into the Idaho Panhandle. This first wave of moisture brought with generally light snow accumulations, but this snow melted and subsequently froze on roadways causing multiple collisions along interstate 90 including a fatal crash near Ritzville. ||A second wave of deeper moisture enhanced along this boundary during the evening and overnight hours and brought heavy accumulations to much of the Columbia Basin and the Palouse of eastern Washington by the morning of February 5th when accumulating snow snow began to  taper off. Snow accumulations of 4 to 6 inches were common near and along this stationary front, with lighter but still significant accumulations of 2 to 4 inches noted across northern Washington and the Cascades east slopes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bluewood ski resort reported 17 inches of new snow accumulation from this storm.

Snow accumulations of 4 to 6 inches were common near and along this stationary front, with lighter but still significant accumulations of 2 to 4 inches noted across northern Washington and the Cascades east slopes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public at the Spruce Springs Campground reported 10 inches of new snow accumulation from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak weather disturbance riding along a stalled front draped along the border between Washington and Canada provided the energy for moist westerly flow to enhance into locally heavy snow during the afternoon of February 6th through the early morning of February 7th. Locally heavy snow accumulations were noted close to the Cascade crest with Holden Village receiving around 10 inches of new snow and the Stevens Pass ski resort reporting over 30 inches. Most locations in the Cascades farther away from the crest received only 3 to 5 inches. However, outside of the mountains the Okanogan Valley was the focus of heavy valley accumulations with local heavy accumulations reported from the Okanogan Highlands as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer at Oroville recorded 5.0 inches of new snow accumulation.

This first wave of moisture brought with generally light snow accumulations, but this snow melted and subsequently froze on roadways causing multiple collisions along interstate 90 including a fatal crash near Ritzville. ||A second wave of deeper moisture enhanced along this boundary during the evening and overnight hours and brought heavy accumulations to much of the Columbia Basin and the Palouse of eastern Washington by the morning of February 5th when accumulating snow snow began to  taper off. Snow accumulations of 4 to 6 inches were common near and along this stationary front, with lighter but still significant accumulations of 2 to 4 inches noted across northern Washington and the Cascades east slopes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer in Lind recorded 5.0 inches of new snow accumulation from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The passage of a cold front produced gusty winds across much of the eastern half of Washington on April 27th. Sustained winds of 25-30 mph with gusts of 40-50 mph were common in areas such as the Palouse, Columbia Basin, West Plains, and Wenatchee Area. The winds produced areas blowing dust with localized visibility reductions in portions of central and eastern Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Washington State Patrol reported that State Route 26 in southwest Whitman County was fully blocked three miles west of Dusty, Washington. An accident involving a semi-truck and two cars occurred due to near zero visibility from blowing dust. There were no injuries. The highway was closed for six hours due to the dust storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The arrival of a strong cold front brought an abrupt change in weather with strong north winds gusting up to 50 mph on April 11th. The winds channeled down the Okanogan Valley into the Columbia Basin creating areas of blowing dust and localized visibility reductions. The strong winds and blowing dust lead to a multiple car crash on Interstate 90 and numerous power outages in the Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong local winds led to power outages in Ritzville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air spilled southward through the Fraser River Valley in mid-January, creating necessary antecedent conditions for the development of lowland snow as a weather system dropped down from Canada/Gulf of Alaska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began falling on the 12th as cold air remain trapped near the surface and weather system passed over the area. This was the first lowland snow event of the season and the year. General snowfall totals ranged from a trace to around 2 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent area of low pressure along the boundary of a stalled Arctic front set the stage for another round of heavy snow accumulations over north Idaho and northeast Washington during the third week of January. Moist air feeding into the area of low pressure impacted primarily the Northeast Mountains zone where the low level wind flow was favorable for upslope enhancement.  Areas on the periphery of the mountains including the Upper Columbia Basin and the Spokane Area zones received generally 1 to 3 inches of snow from this round of precipitation in this active pattern.||Heavy orographic snow also impacted the Blue Mountains of southeast Washington during this period. The western Columbia basin and the East Slopes of the Cascades zone benefited from a precipitation shadow with largely dry conditions or minor accumulations from scattered snow showers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer 6 miles east of Anatone reported 10.0 inches of snow accumulation from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third week of January brought very active winter weather to the Inland Northwest as a series of surface low pressures tracked along a stalled arctic air mass boundary laying near or over eastern Washington and north Idaho. Occasional feeds of Pacific moisture directed into this storm track lead to periodic heavy accumulations of snow across the region through the week.  During the whole week of repeated snow storms many northern valley locations received 2 to 3 feet of snow accumulation with mountain ski resorts reporting 4 to 5 feet of fresh powder.       ||The first surface low pressure system tracked through northern Washington and Idaho on January 11th and 12th. A stationary front followed by a cold front produced a  period of accumulating snow across the region. Only the lowest elevations of the Columbia River and Okanogan River Valleys received minor snow accumulations in the precipitation shadowed lee of the Cascades.   ||Over the East Slopes of the Cascades a persistent Puget Sound Convergence Zone after the cold front passage became focused on the Stevens Pass area for an extended period of time. Stevens Pass recorded 33 inches of snow during this storm. Highway 2 had to be closed at Stevens Pass due to multiple traffic accidents. The BNSF railway line was closed across the pass due to hundreds of trees toppled by heavy wet snow.||Elsewhere across northeast Washington and north Idaho over 25,000 customers lost power due to snow laden trees toppling into power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather spotter in Chattaroy reported 7.0 inches of new snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beginning during the evening of January 15th and continuing through the 16th a warm front pushed north through eastern Washington. The East Slopes of the Cascades and the adjacent Columbia Basin zones were under the deepest moisture feed and received heavy snow accumulations. The remainder of the Columbia Basin received generally 1 to 3 inches of snow accumulation. The warm front stalled near the Canadian border which promoted a longer period of snow over the northern mountains of eastern Washington where another region of heavy accumulations resulted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer in 5 miles south of Wenatchee recorded 4.7 inches of new snow accumulation from this storm.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF SNOHOMISH AND KING COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following closely on the heels of the previous winter storm was another winter storm across the mountainous terrain of western Washington. Periods of heavy snow continued off and on without much in the way of noticeable breaks over the course of a week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Without much break after the previous winter storm, another storm brought heavy snow to the central Cascades consistently from the 10th through the 18th. This storm affected the Passes, with storm total snowfall of around 80 inches at Stevens Pass and around 73 inches at Snoqualmie Pass. I-90 near Snoqualmie Pass was closed several times due to spin-outs and Highway 2 between Gold Bar and Skykomish was closed for several days due to heavy snowfall and downed trees and power lines. Over 200 motorists were stranded as a result of this closure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent area of low pressure along the boundary of a stalled Arctic front set the stage for another round of heavy snow accumulations over north Idaho and northeast Washington during the third week of January. Moist air feeding into the area of low pressure impacted primarily the Northeast Mountains zone where the low level wind flow was favorable for upslope enhancement. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a period of light snow at the very beginning of the month, a more significant winter storm brought heavier snowfall accumulations to the mountains beginning January 4th and continuing through January 8th, prompting the issuance of a Winter Storm Warning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began falling during the very early morning hours of the 4th, with the snow depth near Mount Baker at 58 inches. By 12 am on the 5th, the snow depth deepened to 73 inches. Total snow depth by January 7th reached 95 inches, with snowfall gradually tapering through the day on the 8th.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF WHATCOM AND SKAGIT COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following closely on the heels of the previous winter storm was another winter storm across the mountainous terrain of western Washington. Periods of heavy snow continued off and on without much in the way of noticeable breaks over the course of a week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began to fall during the early morning hours on the 10th, with a quick 20 inches of new snow accumulation 24 hours later around the Mount Baker Ski area. Periods of heavy snow then continued intermittently through the 18th, with approximate storm total snowfall near Mount Baker of 117 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds across western Washington within a series of winter storm systems continued January 12th and 13th, with thousands left without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High wind occurred during a lowland winter weather event. At the peak of the event there were around 96,000 customers without power during this event.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: All of Bainbridge Island lost power at one point during the winter storm on the 12th, with thousands of other people without power in the vicinity. During the morning hours of the 13th, over 10,000 customers were still without power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very active week of frequent snow storms across the Inland Northwest culminated with another warm front passage which concentrated heavy snow over the far western Columbia Basin during the day of January 18th. Snow levels rose during the day as the warm front passed through from south to north allowing partial melting of much of the morning accumulations. The Cascades received generally 3 to 5 inches of wet snow with local accumulations to around 8 inches near the Cascade crest. Elsewhere in eastern Washington, farther away from the deepest moisture feed and also subject to warming during the day generally 1 to 3 inches of wet snow was noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public in Wenatchee reported 6.0 inches of new snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: South winds increased as a cold front approached the area, bringing wind gusts of 60 mph to the south Washington Coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cape Disappointment (82SW1) measured sustained winds of 48 mph with max gusts of 60 mph. The Astoria-Megler Bridge (ODT76) reported sustianed winds of 40 mph with max gusts of 60 mph. Cape Disappointment (82SW1)  messured wind gusts of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple low pressure systems moving southward along the British Columbia coast brought mountain snow to the region over the course of three days. The cold air masses along with the moisture latent systems brought one to three feet of snow to areas along the south Washington Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Calamity SNOTEL (CLJW1 - 2500') reported 6 inches of snow in 11 hours.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF WHATCOM AND SKAGIT COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another weather system brought another round of heavy mountain snow to Washington at the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began to fall during the early morning hours of the 27th and continued fairly consistently through the close of the month, with new snow accumulation of near 50 inches around Mount Baker.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air spilled southward through the Fraser River Valley in mid-January, creating necessary antecedent conditions for the development of lowland snow as a weather system dropped down from Canada/Gulf of Alaska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began falling on the 12th as cold air remain trapped near the surface and weather system passed over the area. This was the first lowland snow event of the season and the year. General snowfall totals ranged from a trace to around 2 inches.

General snowfall totals ranged from a trace to around 2 inches in the Bremerton area, with higher amounts across northern Kitsap County in the 4-7 inch range.

The Cascades received generally 3 to 5 inches of wet snow with local accumulations to around 8 inches near the Cascade crest. Elsewhere in eastern Washington, farther away from the deepest moisture feed and also subject to warming during the day generally 1 to 3 inches of wet snow was noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public in Rock Island reported 5.0 inches of new snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate rain on already saturated soil triggered a hillside to begin slowly creeping and thus undermining State Route 231 near Valley. Traffic was restricted to one lane at the slide site.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A hillside along route 231 near Valley began slowly sliding and compromising the road bed. Temporary traffic lights were installed to restrict traffic to one lane while highway crews stabilized the road bed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of storm systems, each following 12-24 hours in succession during the previous week lead to heavy snow loading on the roof of a forest service building near Blewett Pass in the East Slopes of the Cascades.  The most recent storms were relatively warm and came on the heels of heavy snowfall. This increased the potential for avalanches. On January 23rd, snow fell off the roof of the US Forest Service building and buried an employee. Emergency officials were unable to revive her.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On January 23rd, near Blewett Pass, snow slid off the roof of a US Forest Service building onto an employee, burying her. Emergency officials were unable to revive her. Five inches of snow fell in the area on January 23. At the time of the avalanche, an estimated 28 inches of snow depth was reported at Blewett Pass, and 34 inches at the nearby Grouse Camp SNOTEL.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third week of January brought very active winter weather to the Inland Northwest as a series of surface low pressures tracked along a stalled arctic air mass boundary laying near or over eastern Washington and north Idaho. Occasional feeds of Pacific moisture directed into this storm track lead to periodic heavy accumulations of snow across the region through the week.  During the whole week of repeated snow storms many northern valley locations received 2 to 3 feet of snow accumulation with mountain ski resorts reporting 4 to 5 feet of fresh powder.       ||The first surface low pressure system tracked through northern Washington and Idaho on January 11th and 12th. A stationary front followed by a cold front produced a  period of accumulating snow across the region. Only the lowest elevations of the Columbia River and Okanogan River Valleys received minor snow accumulations in the precipitation shadowed lee of the Cascades.   ||Over the East Slopes of the Cascades a persistent Puget Sound Convergence Zone after the cold front passage became focused on the Stevens Pass area for an extended period of time. Stevens Pass recorded 33 inches of snow during this storm. Highway 2 had to be closed at Stevens Pass due to multiple traffic accidents. The BNSF railway line was closed across the pass due to hundreds of trees toppled by heavy wet snow.||Elsewhere across northeast Washington and north Idaho over 25,000 customers lost power due to snow laden trees toppling into power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer at Boundary Dam recorded 11.0 inches of snow accumulation from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 980 mb low located near 45N/130W along with an attendant warm front moved into the southern Oregon Coast and overran a cold airmass originating from the Columbia River Gorge. This resulted in snow that gradually transitioned to freezing rain in the Gorge on Wednesday night into Thursday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The amounts of snow and ice varied greatly across the Columbia River Gorge, with heaviest amounts in the Central Columbia River Gorge zone. The combination of snow, ice, and wind resulted in the closure of I-84 between Troutdale and Cascade Locks. Based on DOT and spotter reports, 4 to 10 inches fell in the stretch from Corbett to Cascade Locks and Carson, followed by a few hours of light freezing rain.  One to 3 inches fell in the Underwood and Hood River areas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather spotter at Metaline Falls reported 4.0 inches of snow from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 6th a moist warm front passed through eastern Washington bringing heavy snow accumulations to the East Slopes of the Cascades and the Northeast Mountains. In the Okanogan Highlands generally 1 to 3 inches of accumulation fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mount Spokane Ski Resort reported 14 inches of snow from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third week of January brought very active winter weather to the Inland Northwest as a series of surface low pressures tracked along a stalled arctic air mass boundary laying near or over eastern Washington and north Idaho. Occasional feeds of Pacific moisture directed into this storm track lead to periodic heavy accumulations of snow across the region through the week.  During the whole week of repeated snow storms many northern valley locations received 2 to 3 feet of snow accumulation with mountain ski resorts reporting 4 to 5 feet of fresh powder.       ||The first surface low pressure system tracked through northern Washington and Idaho on January 11th and 12th. A stationary front followed by a cold front produced a  period of accumulating snow across the region. Only the lowest elevations of the Columbia River and Okanogan River Valleys received minor snow accumulations in the precipitation shadowed lee of the Cascades.   ||Over the East Slopes of the Cascades a persistent Puget Sound Convergence Zone after the cold front passage became focused on the Stevens Pass area for an extended period of time. Stevens Pass recorded 33 inches of snow during this storm. Highway 2 had to be closed at Stevens Pass due to multiple traffic accidents. The BNSF railway line was closed across the pass due to hundreds of trees toppled by heavy wet snow.||Elsewhere across northeast Washington and north Idaho over 25,000 customers lost power due to snow laden trees toppling into power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer at Stehekin recorded 10.0 inches of new snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 10th, winds increased in association with a passing storm system. Most locations across western Washington experienced breezy conditions, with the Northern Interior and its adjacent waters experiencing the windiest conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds near Port Townsend and Coupeville led to the cancellation of ferries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist and unstable flow from a low pressure system kicked off convection and numerous thunderstorms in southeastern Washington and central Idaho Panhandle on May 18th. |They produced five cold-air funnel clouds in Pullman, Washington over a fifteen minute period. Numerous witnesses confirmed there was no evidence of damage or circulation on the ground. An official National Weather Service survey conducted on May 19th determined that there were no tornadoes. The most well defined cold-air funnel cloud occurred around 3:05 PM approximately 4 miles southeast of Pullman, Washington and dissipated quickly.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported and submitted a picture of a funnel cloud in southeast Pullman, Washington. Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure slowly migrating across western United States kept a moist and unstable southerly flow of moisture into eastern Washington and Idaho on May 19th and 20th. This steered disturbances northward through Eastern Washington. Scattered thunderstorms brought localized ponding and a rock slide. Half an inch of rain fell in Waterville from slow moving cell in far western Douglas County in the late afternoon of May 19th. A few miles to the west along US-2 between Orondo and Waterville, the prolonged period of rainfall led to a rock slide. Scattered thunderstorms across southeastern Washington created a cold-air funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported a brief funnel cloud seen in Ritzville, Washington.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rock slide was reported by Washington State Patrol on Highway US-2 in Pine Canyon between Orondo and Waterville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The passage of a strong cold front brought a line of convection and strong winds in the afternoon and early evening of May 2nd. The fast moving front tracked from the southwest and moved out of northeastern Washington by the mid-evening hours. The outflow boundary produced numerous dust storms ahead of the thunderstorms in far eastern Washington and Columbia Basin. Sustained winds of 25-30 mph with gusts to 40-55 mph created near to zero visibility from the blowing dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local TV news reporter observed less than 1/2 mile visibility in Saint John, Washington from blowing dust.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A utility company reported approximately 45,000 customers without power, mainly in the Cheney and southwest Spokane area, due to branches falling on the power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The arrival of a strong cold front brought an abrupt change in weather with strong north winds gusting up to 50 mph on April 11th. The winds channeled down the Okanogan Valley into the Columbia Basin creating areas of blowing dust and localized visibility reductions. The strong winds and blowing dust lead to a multiple car crash on Interstate 90 and numerous power outages in the Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Washington State Patrol reported a multi-vehicle accident occurred on Interstate 90, 11 miles west of Ritzville. The strong winds resulted in near zero visibility due to the blowing dust. This resulted in a five vehicle accident and 9 non-life threatening injuries. The collision resulted in a subsequent closure of the Interstate for numerous hours following the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought abundant snowfall to the Blue Mountains in SE Washington. Much of the snow melted a few days later as heavy rains moved into the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around a foot of snow fell both at Ski Bluewood and the nearby Touchet Snotel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell over the northwest Blue Mountains of Washington on February 8th, starting early in the morning and ending in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight inches of new snow fell at the Milkshakes Snotel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow on February 4-5, 2020 was followed by a period of heavy rain, February 5-7.  Snow levels rose to near 5000 feet.   The heavy rain and snow melt lead to moderate flooding and several landslides along the East Slopes of the Washington Cascades.   There was record flooding on several rivers, including the Umatilla, Walla Walla and Touchet rivers with widespread flooding of numerous other creeks and streams and rivers across Umatilla, Union and Wallowa Counties of Oregon and Walla Walla and Columbia Counties of Washington. Initial Weather Conditions February 1, 2020, snowpack across along the East Slopes of the Washington Cascades ranged from 80 to 100 percent of normal.   Across the northern Blue Mountains of Oregon and northern Blue mountains of Washington the snowpack was near normal. A weather system brought heavy snow to this region on February 4th and the first part of the day on February 5th with snow amounts from around 10 to 20 inches for locations between 2000 to 4000 feet. During the afternoon of the 5th, snow changed to rain at elevations below 5000 feet.  Selected locations snow amounts for February 4-5, include 8 inches at 2.6 miles SSE of Snoqualmie Pass, 7 inches at Cle Elum, 2 inches at Easton, 12.5 inches at Ski Blue Wood and 2 inches at Dayton in Columbia County. For Oregon, snow amounts include, La Grande 7-10 inches, Summerville 10 inches, La Grande, 5 Miles NNE- 17.5 inches, Meacham 13 inches, Elgin 9.8 N 8 inches, Joseph 6 inches and Wallowa 8 inches. The rain continued over through February 6 and 7th.   Precipitation amounts in the form of rain at the headwaters of the rivers, ranged from 3 to 5 inches.  Rain amounts for the 48 hours ending on the morning of February 8th, for selected locations include 6.31 inches at 2.6 miles SSE of Snoqualmie Pass, 3.27 inches at Easton, 5.83 inches near Lake Cle Elum and 2.17 inches at 8.7 miles NE Dayton.  Snotel sites reported 2 day precipitation amounts of 6.3 inches at Touchet #2 in the Columbia County. There was 1.98 inches over 72 hours at 1.1 NE Walla Walla.   In Oregon, rain amounts for the 48 hours ending on the morning of February 8th for selected locations include:  from Coop Observer, Emigrant Springs ��� 2.82 inches, Union 1.01 inches ,  Cove- 2.48 inches, La Grande - 2.50 inches,  Lostine ��� 1.39 inches.  From CoCoRahs and other public weather spotters, 9.8 N Elgin ��� 5.45 inches, La Grande- 3.02 inches and Wallowa 2.66 inches.  Snotel sites reported 2 day precipitation amounts of 5.9 inches at High Ridge, 3.0 inches at Moss Springs, 1.6 inches at Milkshake. In addition, the snowpack from the preceding day was melted, contributing to the runoff.   Observations show 5 to 7 inches of snow melted from February 5th to February 6th at Easton and Cle Elum.   Home owners in the Lake Cle Elum area noted a loss of 3 to 5 feet of snow An observer located 5 miles north of La Grande reported 17.5 inches of snow fell on February 4-5 and then by the morning of February 6th, the snow depth had decreased to around 8 inches and was depleted during the day on the 6th.   Satellite snowpack images show 1-3 inches of snow water equivalent (or water held in the snowpack) melted out along both the east and west sides of the northern Blue Mountains in Oregon and around the foothills of the Grande Ronde Valley generally between 2000 to 4000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Record high water levels were seen on Upper Mill Creek with extensive damage along upper Mill Creek to homes as well as roads and bridges. A number of residents had to be evacuated via helicopter or 4 wheeler.   There was minimal damage along Mill Creek through Walla Walla as water was diverted through the Mill Creek Flood Project.  Downstream of Walla Walla, there was damaging flooding in College Place with a bridge being washed out near Wallula Road.  In Waitsburg, flood waters from both the Touchet River and Coppei Creek inundated the town as levees were damaged.  60 homes were damaged as well as numerous roads and bridges.  Damage countywide is estimated at 8.77 million dollars. Walla Walla nr Touchet ��� Flood stage is 13.0 feet.  The river rose above flood stage on February 6th at 730 pm, crested at 20.26 feet on February 7th at 445 pm, then fell below flood stage on February 9, 7 am.  Major flooding occurred along the Walla Walla River from the headwaters through Milton Freewater to the confluence with the Columbia River.  There was extensive damage to roads, bridges and damage to levees through Milton- Freewater.   There was widespread flooding of pastures from Milton Freewater to the mouth, with portions of Highway 12 covered by water.

No dollar estimate at this time.

A weather system brought heavy snow to this region on February 4th and the first part of the day on February 5th with snow amounts from around 10 to 20 inches for locations between 2000 to 4000 feet. During the afternoon of the 5th, snow changed to rain at elevations below 5000 feet.  Selected locations snow amounts for February 4-5, include 8 inches at 2.6 miles SSE of Snoqualmie Pass, 7 inches at Cle Elum, 2 inches at Easton, 12.5 inches at Ski Blue Wood and 2 inches at Dayton in Columbia County. For Oregon, snow amounts include, La Grande 7-10 inches, Summerville 10 inches, La Grande, 5 Miles NNE- 17.5 inches, Meacham 13 inches, Elgin 9.8 N 8 inches, Joseph 6 inches and Wallowa 8 inches. The rain continued over through February 6 and 7th.   Precipitation amounts in the form of rain at the headwaters of the rivers, ranged from 3 to 5 inches.  Rain amounts for the 48 hours ending on the morning of February 8th, for selected locations include 6.31 inches at 2.6 miles SSE of Snoqualmie Pass, 3.27 inches at Easton, 5.83 inches near Lake Cle Elum and 2.17 inches at 8.7 miles NE Dayton.  Snotel sites reported 2 day precipitation amounts of 6.3 inches at Touchet #2 in the Columbia County. There was 1.98 inches over 72 hours at 1.1 NE Walla Walla.   In Oregon, rain amounts for the 48 hours ending on the morning of February 8th for selected locations include:  from Coop Observer, Emigrant Springs ��� 2.82 inches, Union 1.01 inches ,  Cove- 2.48 inches, La Grande - 2.50 inches,  Lostine ��� 1.39 inches.  From CoCoRahs and other public weather spotters, 9.8 N Elgin ��� 5.45 inches, La Grande- 3.02 inches and Wallowa 2.66 inches.  Snotel sites reported 2 day precipitation amounts of 5.9 inches at High Ridge, 3.0 inches at Moss Springs, 1.6 inches at Milkshake. In addition, the snowpack from the preceding day was melted, contributing to the runoff.   Observations show 5 to 7 inches of snow melted from February 5th to February 6th at Easton and Cle Elum.   Home owners in the Lake Cle Elum area noted a loss of 3 to 5 feet of snow An observer located 5 miles north of La Grande reported 17.5 inches of snow fell on February 4-5 and then by the morning of February 6th, the snow depth had decreased to around 8 inches and was depleted during the day on the 6th.   Satellite snowpack images show 1-3 inches of snow water equivalent (or water held in the snowpack) melted out along both the east and west sides of the northern Blue Mountains in Oregon and around the foothills of the Grande Ronde Valley generally between 2000 to 4000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Naches near Naches- Flood stage is 17.8 feet.  The river rose above flood stage on February 7, 4 am, crested at 18.6 feet on February 7th at 130 pm, then fell below flood stage on February 8th at 430 am. Minor flooding was observed in low areas along river.

There was record flooding on several rivers, including the Umatilla, Walla Walla and Touchet rivers with widespread flooding of numerous other creeks and streams and rivers across Umatilla, Union and Wallowa Counties of Oregon and Walla Walla and Columbia Counties of Washington. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and moist air mass moved northward from north-central Oregon into eastern Washington on May 30th. With an afternoon of convective heating, there were numerous strong to severe thunderstorms developing and moving across the state during the late afternoon hours. With a very unstable and moist atmosphere, these thunderstorms brought very strong winds, with some gusts up to 50 - 70 mph, across much of the central and southeastern portions of the state. There was some small, pea sized hail and lightning. However, these severe cells brought some significant wind damage to homes, public areas, and roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported damage to roof, windows, and a fence from strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The passage of a strong cold front brought a line of convection and strong winds in the afternoon and early evening of May 2nd. The fast moving front tracked from the southwest and moved out of northeastern Washington by the mid-evening hours. The outflow boundary produced numerous dust storms ahead of the thunderstorms in far eastern Washington and Columbia Basin. Sustained winds of 25-30 mph with gusts to 40-55 mph created near to zero visibility from the blowing dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS employee observed near zero visibility due to blowing dust along Highway US-95, north of Colfax, Washington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow over the Blue Mountains and Kittitas Valley February 15-17 with 8-11 inches of snow in the mountains and 5-6 inches of snow in the valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: New snow of 5-6 inches across the valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds associated with a cold front caused very low visibility in blowing dust along I-82 south of Kennewick. This resulted in numerous accidents and a closure of the interstate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds associated with a cold front resulted in extremely low visibility due to blowing dust along I-82 south of Kennewick. This resulted in numerous motor vehicle accidents and subsequent closure of the Interstate. 5 people were hospitalized with non life threatening injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions over central Washington in July created dry fuel conditions across the region. The Road 11 Wildfire developed on July 11th initially burning northward. A change in wind direction pushed the fire eastward threatening the town of Mansfield and requiring the closure of State Route 172. Level 3 evacuation notices were issued for Mansfield as the fire approached. Several outbuildings were destroyed, one home damaged, and nearly 10,000 acres of grass, sage, and wheat were burned. State assistance was mobilized to help local firefighters and contain the wildfire by late July 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Road 11 brush fire consumed approximately 10,000 acres of grassland and wheat. Several outbuildings were lost and there was damage to one home. The cost of firefighting resources to contain the blaze was calculated at 980,000 dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Greenhouse Wildfire started on July 23rd 1 mile southwest of Nespelem on the Colville Indian Reservation. The fire was ignited due to unknown causes, but grew rapidly under very warm and dry conditions. Level 3 evacuations occurred for many and included the inmates at the Colville Tribal Corrections Facility. Many structures were threatened and several damaged. Wind and low relative humidity fueled the fire which consumed 5,320 acres of timber and grass before contained July 30. The firefighting resources necessary to bring the fire under control were estimated at 3.4 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Nespelem Transfer Station and the Colville Tribal Recycling Center were severely damaged.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF SNOHOMISH AND KING COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of storm systems traversed the Pacific Northwest in mid November, resulting in heavy mountain snow and travel impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow began to fall in the Central Cascades around 7pm on Nov 12. By 11pm, WSDOT stated the I-90 was closed in both directions around MP 47/Denny Creek due to multiple vehicle spinouts. Around midnight, WSDOT announced the reopening of westbound lanes with chains required. By 4am on Nov 13, I-90 was open in both directions, with snow continuing and overall lighter accumulations.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow began to accumulate over the North Cascades the morning of the 18th. Within a 24 hour period, the Mount Baker Ski Resort has reported 21 inches of news snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system brought light showers and breezy winds to much of Central Washington. Strong afternoon winds, with gusts from 35 - 50 mph, were reported along the Columbia River in Chelan and Douglas Counties. No widespread damage was reported. Rain was light and many locations only received a few sprinkles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local utility company reported a tree blown into powerlines due to strong winds caused a small power outage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A new winter system moved in as a trough dropped into the Pacific Northwest on December 21st. With the accompanying atmospheric river, this system had substantial moisture with it bringing a wet and windy day ahead. Most of Eastern Washington was still too warm for snow, but there was moderate snowfall in the east Cascade slopes. Throughout the remainder of the region, locations received 24 hour rainfall totals up to 0.5 - 1.0. ||In addition to wet conditions, the evening of the 21st into the early morning hours of the 22nd were windy, with damaging winds in the Palouse. The strongest winds were in far Eastern Washington, from Spokane to Pullman, and Cascade slopes. There were sustained winds 20 - 30 mph with gusts to 35 - 50 mph for over a 12 hour period. It was windy for much of the region, but much lighter with gusts to approximately 20 mph. Numerous power outages occurred in Chelan, Whitman, and Stevens counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer, 3 miles southeast of Winthrop, recorded 7.1 inches of new snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread, winter system brought snow to much of Eastern Washington on December 30th and 31st. The initial snowfall began overnight into the early morning hours of the 30th. This resulted in difficult morning commutes and numerous collisions and spin offs, especially in the Spokane area on Interstate 90 to Ritzville. Significant snow fell throughout Eastern Washington with the greatest totals, up to 8 - 12 inches, in northeastern Washington north of Highway 2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter, 2 miles east of Five Mile, recorded 10.1 inches of new snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist occluded front pushed across Washington and brought the next winter system late December 16th into the early morning hours of December 17th. With the easterly to southeasterly flow, this resulted in enhanced upslope where precipitation amounts were enhanced with the frontal passage. There was moderate to heavy snowfall, which kept to the eastern slopes of the northern Cascades, Wenatchee area, and Methow Valley, bringing 5 to 10 inches of new snow accumulation overnight. Light snowfall and low land rain fell across the remainder of eastern Washington and Idaho Panhandle region due to warmer temperatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer, 1 mile north-northwest of Plain, recorded 7.0 inches of new snow accumulation.

Significant precipitable water and a surface low's colder air provided conditions for snow and rain/snow mixes across the region. Only 1-2 inches fell in most low lands, but into the Spokane area and eastern North Cascade slopes up to 4-7 inches of snow was recorded. ||The road conditions began to deteriorate when the started snow falling that evening, which led to a minimum of 33 crashes in the Spokane area. In addition to slippery roadways from snow, highway troopers responded crashes due to heavy, freezing fog slick roadways across eastern Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CO-OP observer station in Stehekin recorded 5.0 inches of new snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A new winter system moved in as a trough dropped into the Pacific Northwest on December 21st. With the accompanying atmospheric river, this system had substantial moisture with it bringing a wet and windy day ahead. Most of Eastern Washington was still too warm for snow, but there was moderate snowfall in the east Cascade slopes. Throughout the remainder of the region, locations received 24 hour rainfall totals up to 0.5 - 1.0. ||In addition to wet conditions, the evening of the 21st into the early morning hours of the 22nd were windy, with damaging winds in the Palouse. The strongest winds were in far Eastern Washington, from Spokane to Pullman, and Cascade slopes. There were sustained winds 20 - 30 mph with gusts to 35 - 50 mph for over a 12 hour period. It was windy for much of the region, but much lighter with gusts to approximately 20 mph. Numerous power outages occurred in Chelan, Whitman, and Stevens counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public, in Mazama, reported 9.0 inches of new snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front produced strong northerly wind gusts of 40-65 mph across Oregon and Washington during the afternoon and early evening of September 7, 2020.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered trees and powerlines down across the county due to high winds. Some power outages reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved south across Washington and Oregon on September 7th, 2020 producing strong northerly winds. These winds kicked up dust and reduced visibility to 1/4 mile at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pasco ASOS (KPSC) reported visibility less than 1/4 mile due to blowing dust and smoke. Visibilities were reduced for much of the day into the early evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire driven by strong winds closed I-82 in both directions from MP 82 to MP 88.|No events found.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Interstate 82 shut down in both directions between MP 82 and MP 88 due to fast moving wildfire east of Prosser. 2 homes and 15 outbuildings destroyed near Basin City. Railroad Bridge that crosses the Yakima River near Prosser was torched.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of storm system traversed the Pacific Northwest in mid November, yielding rough surf combined with a period of high tides. Coastal flooding became a primary concern in these conditions. Coastal flooding was most destructive around Westport on the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large waves in tandem with King Tides led to coastal flooding along the Central Coast of Washington on Nov 15. Numerous reports and pictures/videos were posted on Twitter, with flooding noticeable in parking lots and within business districts. Westport appeared to be hardest hit, with several businesses experiencing flooding in the Marina District. Hazardous ocean conditions led to copious amounts of gravel to build up over Half Moon Bay. This helped seawater flow directly into the business district as well as inundate Westhaven Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Breezy winds and unsettled seas continued over portions of Western Washington's interior waters after a series of strong systems moved through several days prior. These conditions led a rouge wave near Jetty Island near Everett, WA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 9pm on Nov 17, a rogue wave caused a Tulalip Police Department Fisheries vessel that was assisting another vessel to capsize near Jetty Island. Search and rescue efforts were hampered by stormy weather and heavy seas with debris. One person was rescued shortly before midnight, with another person initially listed as missing. Recovery efforts continued, with the missing person never found and presumed to be dead.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of storm systems traversed the Pacific Northwest in mid November, with breezy to locally conditions experienced across lowland locations on November 13. While wind damage was not extensive in any particular location, numerous trees and powerlines were downed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds across Island County on Nov 13 led to the temporary closure of several state parks including: Deception Pass and Fort Ebey. Numerous trees and powerlines were reported down across Whidbey and Fidalgo Islands in Island County. The Park was determined to be unsafe due to these conditions and was closed by WA State Parks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very dry and critical fire conditions over Eastern Washington during July and August produced dry fuels across the region. These fuels, with an incoming cold front's strong winds, created an outbreak of wildfires, blowing smoke, and dust late September 6th - 7th. These conditions ignited over 20 wildfires which destroyed hundreds of thousand acres of land, crops, and homes. 80 percent of the small town of Malden, and nearby Pine City, burned during a fast moving wildfire. There were over 500 miles of Interstate 90, state highways, and roads closed due to fires, low visibility from smoke and dust, and blocked from downed trees. One fatality and two severe injuries occurred when trying to escape one of the wildfires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Customs Road Wildfire, 1 mile west of Curlew, was started by human causes on September 7th. There were 11 structures lost. The wildfire grew to 2,208 acres, driven by wind and topography, before largely contained by mid-September. The cost of fighting the fire was calculated at 850,000 dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The passage of a frontal boundary brought strong winds in the afternoon of September 25th. The winds surged across eastern Washington bringing gusts to 40 mph in some areas of the Columbia Basin, Palouse, Okanogan Valley, and Waterville Plateau. These winds produced several dust storms with near zero visibility. The strong winds created blowing dust leading to multiple car accidents and a closed state highway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Department of Highways reported State Highway 26 closed and multiple collisions due to blowing dust from strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple large wildfires in Washington, Oregon, and surrounding states produced copious quantities of dense smoke throughout the month of September. Due to poor ventilation and a gradual return to southwest winds, there were increasing amounts of wildfire smoke to Washington. The smoke from these fires severely degraded air quality across much of eastern Washington in mid-September. Numerous communities, especially in the Spokane area, suffered long periods of very unhealthy to hazardous air quality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An air quality sensor in Spokane recorded unhealthy air quality from September 12th through 19th. It spiked to continuous hazardous conditions on September 13th through 15th. The arrival of a cold front on September 19th brought showers and westerly winds which ventilated the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Palmer Fire, 7 miles southwest of Oroville, started on August 18th. Shifting high winds created extreme fire behavior and accelerated rapid fire growth. Passing frontal boundaries assisted in strengthening winds with gusts to 48 mph. Prior to containment, many homes were threatened and Level 3 evacuation orders were issued for those in  Loomis and Nighthawk. There is no known destruction of structures. The fire was contained by September 8th with a total 17,988 acres destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Palmer Wildfire grew and burned a total 17,988 acres of timber and brush.  The cost of firefighting resources to contain the blaze was calculated at nine million dollars. No structures were lost, but the fire threatened many in the towns of Loomis and Nighthawk.

There were over 500 miles of Interstate 90, state highways, and roads closed due to fires, low visibility from smoke and dust, and blocked from downed trees. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Around Labor Day and through the first full week of September, an anomalous upper level ridge set up over the Northwest US, with a thermal trough developing along the Pacific Northwest Coast with high pressure east of the Cascades at the surface. This setup was responsible for the development of strong easterly winds and high wildfire/fire spread threat. The generation of numerous wildfires then led to a prolonged period of poor air quality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Anomalous upper level high pressure combined with surface low pressure near the WA Coast and high pressure east of the WA Cascades led to the favorable set up for wildfires across the area. With the development of strong Cascade gap winds from the east, the Sumner Grade fire was ignited as strong winds downed trees onto power lines. The fire burned 800 acres before becoming contained. The fire causes structural damage, school closings, power outages, and evacuations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Around Labor Day and through the first full week of September, an anomalous upper level ridge set up over the Northwest US, with a thermal trough developing along the Pacific Northwest Coast with high pressure east of the Cascades at the surface. This setup was responsible for the development of strong easterly winds and high wildfire/fire spread threat. The generation of numerous wildfires then led to a prolonged period of poor air quality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Anomalous upper level high pressure over the Northwest US in combination with surface low pressure along the WA Coast and high pressure east of the WA Cascades led to the development of favorable wildfire conditions. This particular set up led to the development of strong east winds through the Cascades gaps amidst dry antecedent conditions. In Graham, WA several brushfires were initially reported as windy conditions knocked over power lines. These brushfires later combined to form a larger wildfire that led to evacuations of local residents, and eventually structural damage to local homes and buildings. The fire burned 275 acres before becoming contained on Sept 13.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Around Labor Day and through the first full week of September, an anomalous upper level ridge set up over the Northwest US, with a thermal trough developing along the Pacific Northwest Coast with high pressure east of the Cascades at the surface. This setup was responsible for the development of strong easterly winds and high wildfire/fire spread threat. The generation of numerous wildfires then led to a prolonged period of poor air quality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During a period of strong easterly winds, three vessels moored on the outside breakwater at Seabeck marina in Hood Canal. The wind and wave action caused them to impact the dock. The damage included severe denting of hull and upper deck, one of the vessels was holed and took on water,  and one vessel has its engines moved about 3/4 in causing misalignment of prop shafts. All vessels had damage to the Starboard side.

The town of Burien reported tree branches falling on wires, resulting in a power outage affecting 5300 customers. Seattle City Light reported a power outage near Beacon Hill affecting 5000 customers.||Several brush fires were also reported during this period of strong wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After an anomalous period of strong offshore flow and warm, dry weather, numerous wildfires ignited across the Pacific Northwest. As the large scale weather pattern began to shift into mid September, conditions become favorable for the advection of smoke across most of Washington. This results in a period of poor air quality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ignition of multiple wildfires across the Pacific Northwest around Labor Day led to a period of poor air quality by mid-September as dense smoke combined with low level moisture and fog. Alaska Airlines suspended all flights to and from airports in Seattle and Portland, a Seattle Mariners game vs the San Fransisco Giants was postponed due to poor air quality, and all city of Seattle beaches, boat ramps, parks, and play fields closed amidst poor air quality around Sept 15. Many COVID19 sites also closed due to poor air quality. Dense smoke, haze, and poor air quality remained a problem for days across all of Washington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved to the south Washington and north and central Oregon coasts resulting in high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cape Disappointment sensor (82SW1) measured a sustained wind speed of 61 mph with a gust to 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system over the northeast Pacific generated large waves that moved to the Oregon and Washington coasts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Shoalwater Bay Berm near Tokeland was damaged by high surf and unusually high tides. The Grays Harbor buoy (46211) reported significant waves ranging from 18 feet at 17 seconds to 23 feet at 15 seconds. The Toke Point tide gauge recorded a high tide of 12.2 feet at 1218PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong south winds, large surf, and high astronomical tides resulted in tidal overflow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water across Highway 101 in Raymond around the time of high tide. The high tide at Toke Point was 12.2 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system off the Washington coast brought strong southerly winds, large waves resulting in tidal overflow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A road was flooded in Deep River.  A large field was flooded near where the Naselle River meets Willapa Bay.  The high tide at Toke Point was 12.79 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In late July, there were many days of gusty winds and record heat that brought elevated fire spread, especially to the Columbia Basin and Okanogan Valley. Three new wildfires were ignited between July 27-30th: Anglin, Green, and Coulee Meadows. They all quickly grew due to high temperatures, winds, and low relative humidity. The combined three wildfires destroyed over 4000 acres of land and three buildings. No human lives were lost or injured. The wildfires were contained within a few days and during the first week of August. Coulee Meadows was the last, fully contained on August 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Anglin Fire, 8 miles east of Tonasket, started on July 27th by human causes and burned a total 1,992 acres of timber before contained on August 1. Three structures were destroyed: one human residence and two out buildings. The cost of the firefighting resources required to contain the fire was calculated at 1.8 million dollars.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE WASHINGTON CASCADES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and cold front passage followed by a deep Pacific low moving across the region starting during the day on Dec. 29th through Dec. 31st produced heavy snow along the Washington Cascade east slopes and along the Northwest Blue Mountains of Washington, which resulted in accumulations of 12 inches and more being reported in those areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A co-op observer 2 miles SSE of Snoqualmie Pass, WA recorded 14 inches of new snow between 6am PST on 12/30 and 6am PST on 12/31. A Cocorahs observer 6 miles NW of Easton, WA measured 14 inches of new snow between 7am PST on 12/30 and 7am PST on 12/31.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The passage of a strong cold front brought a line of strong winds in the early morning of May 6th. The winds surged north-northeast with the front impacting much of eastern Washington, Columbia Basin, and eastern Cascade Mountain slopes. This produced a dust storm and wind damage from the sudden, but short lived burst of strong winds. Sustained winds of 25-30 mph with gusts to 40-55 mph created less than one mile visibility from the blowing dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported blowing dust and strong winds between Colfax and Pullman along US-195. The visibility was estimated to be less than 1 mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very active storm system in the north Pacific generated large swell directed at the Washington and Oregon coasts that lasted for several days. Additionally a combination of high surf and high astronomical tides contributed to tidal overflow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Buoy 46041, Cape Elizabeth, off the Washington coast reported wave heights exceeding 20 feet at 17 seconds at 2040PST on the 13th. Wave heights dropped some on the 14th and 15th. Waves then picked up again on the 16th, this time peaking at 25 feet at 17 seconds at 2240PST on the 16th. Waves subsided below high surf criteria on the morning of the 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very active storm system in the north Pacific generated large swell directed at the Washington and Oregon coasts that lasted for several days. Additionally a combination of high surf and high astronomical tides contributed to tidal overflow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tide gage at Toke Point, WA reported water levels exceeding 11 feet during the higher high tides from the 13th through the 15th. The water levels were 11.1 feet at 1106PST on the 13th, 11.2 feet at 1154PST on the 14th, and 11.7 feet at 1236PST on the 15th. Water levels at this height usually results in road closures for low lying areas.

There were Level 1 evacuation notices, but no structures or homes were damaged or lost. The cost of the firefighting resources required to contain the fire was calculated at 250,000 dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Badger Lake, Williams Lake, and Chapman Lake, located 10 miles to the southwest of Cheney, were threatened by numerous wildfires started on August 16th. A total of 333 acres were burned by ignitions started by unknown causes. With many dry fuels, strong winds, and low relative humidity, the Badger Lake fire was not fully contained until August 21st. The three other fires were much smaller and contained shortly after crews arrived. While there were many structures threatened, no known homes or out buildings were lost.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Badger Lake Fire, 10 miles southwest of Cheney, started on August 18 by unknown causes and burned a total 244 acres of timber before contained on August 21. There were Level 3 evacuation notices and approximately 30 structures threatened, but none were damaged or lost. The cost of the firefighting resources required to contain the fire was calculated at 1.3 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Lower Coyote Creek Fire began on August 9th, 2 miles to the southwest of Nespelem, due to an unknown cause. The wildfire burned directly west of the Greenhouse Fire from late July along the Columbia River. The fire burned 2,582 acres of grass and timber, with numerous road closures, but no known structures lost.  The wildfire was fully contained on August 15.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cost of firefighting resources to contain the blaze was calculated at 2.6 million dollars.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding of parking lots and at least one business in North Cove/Washaway Beach. The high tide at Toke Point was 12.79 feet.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Buoy 46041, Cape Elizabeth, off the Washington coast reported wave heights exceeding 17 feet at 21 seconds at 1140PST on the 7th, peaking at 25 feet at 21 seconds. Waves subsided below high surf criteria on the morning of the 8th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Bartholomew Wildfire began on August 27 about 1 mile east of Medical Lake along Highway 902. The wildfire burned an approximate 200 acres of grass, shrub, and forest lands. An additional 50 acres were lost on the Fairchild Air Force Base. The structural damage occurred on August 27 and 28, but full containment did not occur until August 29. One residence and three outbuildings were destroyed by the fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bartholomew Fire destroyed four structures: one residence and three outbuildings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Chief Timothy Wildfire was located 5 miles west of Clarkston, WA in the Snake River Canyon. It burned a total 1,400 acres of rangeland and no structures were threatened. The cost of firefighting resources to contain the blaze was estimated at 339,000 dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pullman Radio reported that the Chief Timothy Wildfire started when a motorhome traveling along the Snake River caught fire. There were no injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Colockum Road Wildfire was discovered on July 24th near the town of Malaga, 10 miles southeast of Wenatchee. Under very warm and dry conditions, the fire's spread was fueled by several days of breezy winds. There were over 100 homes and buildings threatened, which initiated a level 3 evacuation. Several crops and orchard were threatened as well, but no losses. The fire was contained by July 28th with a total 2,967 acres destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Colockum Road Wildfire grew and eventually burned 2,967 acres of brush and grassland. No structures were lost, but the fire threatened over 100 homes and several crops and orchards. The cost of firefighting resources to contain the blaze was calculated at 2.4 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An end of the year Pacific storm system brought breezy to locally windy condition to portions of the Western Washington lowlands on December 30.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fort Ebey State Park was forced to close for a period of time on December 30 due to numerous downed trees, per WA State Parks. The ferry service from 8am-1030am from Port Townsend and Coupeville were cancelled due to high wind and rough seas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved into the area on October 13, bringing gusty winds to much of Western Washington. Winds gusted to between 45-55 mph across lowland locations, including areas along the Coast, areas around Puget Sound, and northward towards the United Stated and Canada border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous power outages were reported as the area faced its first wind storm of the season. A man in his 30s was struck and killed by a falling tree while clearing debris in his yard on Key Peninsula near Tacoma, WA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An offshore trough swung inland into the Inland Northwest on June 12th. With it, a shortwave took on a strong negative tilt and moved in a moist, very unstable air mass.  This brought strong to locally severe thunderstorms through far eastern Washington and southern Idaho Panhandle. A few large cells moved through the southeastern counties of Asotin, Garfield, and Whitman in the afternoon and early evening hours. These storms produced hail, winds with gusts to 25 mph, and isolated lightning. Hail was the largest impact with numerous reports of hail from 0.50 to 1.0 inch in Asotin County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported hail with a diameter of 1 inch from thunderstorms near Clarkston.

This brought strong to locally severe thunderstorms through far eastern Washington and southern Idaho Panhandle. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CO-OP observer reported hail with a diameter of 1 inch from passing thunderstorms in Anatone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved into the area on October 13, bringing gusty winds to much of Western Washington. Winds gusted to between 45-55 mph across lowland locations, including areas along the Coast, areas around Puget Sound, and northward towards the United Stated and Canada border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds led to downed trees and powerlines blocking both lanes of traffic along SR-3. Washington State Patrol closed the road in both directions for a period of time.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF SNOHOMISH AND KING COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong cyclonic flow and the passage of a shortwave trough, accompanied by a strong mid level jet moved into Washington during the early morning hours of the 21st. Large scale lift aided in the development of precipitation across the northern half of Western Washington. A period of heavy snow brought accumulating snowfall to the North and Central Cascades, especially to the Central Cascades around Stevens Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow within a convergence zone in the central Cascades led to accumulations of around 6.5 inches within a twelve hour period at Stevens Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong Pacific storm system produced locally damaging winds in the state of Washington the afternoon of Oct. 13, 2020.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power outages due to strong winds in Sunnyside, WA and Toppenish, WA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed across eastern Washington on October 13th bringing strong, damaging winds. With the afternoon sun, the stronger winds aloft mixed down and brought strengthening gusts across central and southeastern Washington. A high wind warning and wind advisory were issued for 1400 to 2300 PDT for a majority of the region. Sustained winds reached 35 - 45 mph with gusts reaching up to 50 - 60 mph in the Columbia Basin, Palouse, and West Plains. ||Across these areas, there were thousands of people without power due to fallen trees and downed power lines. Blowing dust created near zero visibility due to strong winds in the Columbia Basin, which lead to a fatality from a collision. The strong winds created moderate damage to a local school.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Avista Utilities and Inland Power reported over 8000 customers without power due to strong winds in the greater Spokane area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado, with estimated wind speeds of 90 MPH occurred near Neilton in Grays Harbor County, WA. The damage was limited to trees across Moclips Highway and a nearby forest service road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Quinault Indian Nation Fire official reported that at approximately 5:20 AM he came upon 10 downed trees (1-2.5 feet in diameter) across Moclips Highway (S-26) at approximately mile marker 18. Photographs were provided of the downed trees. Subsequent reports and imagery from a trained spotter identified additional damage approximately 0.5 miles to the northeast across a forest service road. No injuries or other impacts were reported.||An evaluation of the photographic evidence from local officials and the trained spotter combined with radar signatures from the event are consistent with a brief tornado. Based on the location and path length the tornado most likely touched down at 4:40am and lasted for less than 2 minutes, traveling 0.50 miles with a maximum width of 30 yards. The degree of tree damage is consistent with peak winds of approximately 90 MPH, rating it as an EF-1 tornado on the Enhanced Fujita scale.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF PIERCE AND LEWIS COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid level disturbance passed over Western Washington the night of October 10, providing enough ascent to generate areas of precipitation. Snow levels and temperatures were low enough across the Southern Cascades, particularly Mount Rainier, for snow to accumulate in a short period of time into the morning hours of October 11.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of heavy snow fell across the Southern Cascades of Washington beginning the evening of the 10th and continuing through the morning hours of October 11. Paradise at Mount Rainier received 8.5 inches of new snow with this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front passing through the Pacific Northwest brought mostly dry and windy��conditions across the eastern half of Washington on June��24th. By early afternoon, isolated��showers and thunderstorms moved into the northern��mountains. With afternoon��convection, there were numerous��strong thunderstorm cells forming in Stevens and Pend Oreille counties. These thunderstorms brought gusty winds, brief heavy downpours, isolated hail, and a funnel cloud. There were reports of hail sizing from 0.25 to 1.5 inches in diameter. With the hail, some locations received an additional rain up to 1 inch that afternoon. The winds, with gusts up to 55 mph, resulted in trees down. Other locations within eastern Washington only received a trace to 0.03 inch of rain. There were widespread winds with gusts reaching 25-40 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported hail with a diameter of 1.5 inches from thunderstorms near Marcus.

These thunderstorms brought gusty winds, brief heavy downpours, isolated hail, and a funnel cloud. There were reports of hail sizing from 0.25 to 1.5 inches in diameter. With the hail, some locations received an additional rain up to 1 inch that afternoon. The winds, with gusts up to 55 mph, resulted in trees down. Other locations within eastern Washington only received a trace to 0.03 inch of rain. There were widespread winds with gusts reaching 25-40 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported a funnel cloud from State Route 20 near Blueslide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major severe weather event by Pacific Northwest standards occurred over central and northeast Oregon and southeast Washington on May 30th. A powerful upper level storm system moved across the area during the afternoon and evening helping to trigger severe thunderstorms. The airmass was unusually unstable for this area of the country with surface dewpoints well into the 60s. With strong upper level winds in place the stage was set for numerous fast moving intense storms through the afternoon and early evening. Numerous reports of damaging winds were received across southeast Washington as the storms moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees down.
